Look Where It Matters:

Training-Free Ultra-HR Remote Sensing VQA via Adaptive Zoom Search

Yunqi Zhou1* ‚ÄÉ‚ÄäChengjie Jiang2* ‚ÄÉ‚ÄäChun Yuan2 ‚ÄÉ‚ÄäJing Li3‚ÄÝ

1 Central University of Finance and Economics ‚ÄÇ‚ÄÑ‚Ää2 Tsinghua University ‚ÄÇ‚ÄÑ‚Ää3 East China Normal University

Abstract

With advances in satellite constellations, sensor technologies, and imaging pipelines, ultra-high-resolution (Ultra-HR) remote sensing imagery is becoming increasingly widespread. However, current remote sensing foundation models are ill-suited to such inputs: full-image encoding exhausts token and memory budgets, while resize-based preprocessing loses fine-grained and answer-critical details. In this context, guiding the model look where it matters before prediction becomes crucial. Therefore, we present ZoomSearch, a training-free, plug-and-play pipeline that decouples ‚Äòwhere to look‚Äô from ‚Äòhow to answer‚Äô for Ultra-HR Remote Sensing Visual Question Answering (RS-VQA). ZoomSearch combines Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search, which performs a hierarchical search over image patches to localize query-relevant regions, with Layout-Aware Patch Reassembly, which reorganizes the selected patches into a compact, layout-faithful canvas. We conduct comprehensive experiments on Ultra-HR RS-VQA benchmarks MME-RealWorld-RS and LRS-VQA, comparing against (i) strong general foundation models, (ii) remote sensing foundation models, (iii) Ultra-HR RS-VQA methods, and (iv) plug-and-play search-based VQA methods. When integrated with LLaVA-ov, ZoomSearch attains state-of-the-art accuracy across diverse tasks, improving the LLaVA-ov baseline by 26.3% on LRS-VQA and 114.8% on MME-RealWorld-RS. Meanwhile, it achieves much higher inference efficiency, outperforming prior search-based methods by 20‚àº\sim44% in speed. (ii) Overall comparison on LRS-VQA. ZoomSearch+LLaVA-ov surpasses all other thirteen methods, achieving an accuracy that is 12.5% higher than the previous best GPT-4o and 26.3% higher than the LLaVA-ov baseline.

1 Introduction

With advances in low-Earth-orbit satellite constellations¬Ý[vleo], high-performance sensors¬Ý[toth2016remotesensing], and end-to-end imaging pipelines¬Ý[wulder], Ultra-HR imagery has become increasingly prevalent in remote sensing¬Ý[dota, isaid, HRVQA, xlrs]. Such data underpin applications in urban monitoring, environmental surveillance, disaster response and so on¬Ý[understanding, efficient]. Historically, experts manually interpret these images, which is time-consuming and labor-intensive. Against this backdrop, remote sensing foundation models¬Ý[geochat, skysense, skysense-o, skysense++, skysense2, geoground, lhrs, earthgpt, grasp, vhm] have emerged to enable automated analysis and progressed rapidly. They exhibit strong generalization and are now widely applied to RS-VQA, including perception-oriented queries and object-counting tasks.

However, most of them are trained with fixed low input resolutions (e.g., 336√ó336336{\times}336 in VHM¬Ý[vhm]; 504√ó504504{\times}504 in GeoChat¬Ý[geochat]) and are not directly compatible with Ultra-HR imagery (long side 2‚àº202{\sim}20K). When applied to Ultra-HR inputs, practitioners typically adopt two handling strategies, both of which are inadequate: (i) resize-based preprocessing discards fine structures and small objects that are often answer-critical; and (ii) tile-and-concatenate dramatically increases the visual-token budget (e.g., encoding an 8K image at VHM scale requires ‚àº\sim300K visual tokens), resulting in out-of-memory failures and prohibitive inference costs.

In response to these limitations, recent studies have begun to tackle Ultra-HR RS-VQA along two main directions. The first is token pruning¬Ý[lrsvqa, geollava8k], which compresses inputs by removing background or low-information regions. Although effective, such methods typically still rely on downsampling or pooling to maintain computational feasibility of attention, thereby sacrificing detail. The second is retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)¬Ý[rag, imagerag, rsrag], which enriches context by retrieving similar patches from labeled databases. However, its effectiveness hinges on retrieval reliability and database‚Äìimage alignment, both of which can be brittle in remote sensing settings. Despite their distinct limitations, these approaches yield a common and valuable insight: models must look where it matters.

Motivated by this premise, we recast the question of ‚Äòwhere to look‚Äô as an explicit search problem before answer generation. To develop a complete search-based pipeline, we pose two design questions: (i) what is an appropriate search unit for Ultra-HR imagery; and (ii) once relevant regions are identified, how should they be reassembled for answer prediction by a foundation model? To address these questions, we conducted pilot studies on LRS-VQA¬Ý[lrsvqa] using LLaVA-ov¬Ý[llavaov] as the base model.

For the first question, we contrasted three search policies: (i) a fine-tile policy that evaluates fixed 224√ó224224\times 224 tiles directly; (ii) a coarse-patch policy that partitions each image into a fixed 3√ó33\times 3 grid and selects among those patches; and (iii) a hierarchical policy that recursively splits the currently selected region into a 3√ó33\times 3 grid and continues to zoom in until sufficient evidence is obtained. Owing to substantial variation in object scales across Ultra-HR remote sensing scenes, the hierarchical policy consistently yields superior end-to-end accuracy. To address the second question, we compared three strategies for reassembling the selected regions: (i) sequential concatenation, which places patches in a fixed raster order from upper left to lower right; (ii) relative-layout preserving, which compacts the canvas while maintaining inter-patch spatial relations; and (iii) relative & global-layout preserving, which additionally preserves each patch‚Äôs global orientation with respect to the original image. The third strategy consistently achieves the best results, indicating that retaining spatial cues at both local and global levels is crucial for reliable Ultra-HR RS-VQA.

Guided by the above preliminary studies, we propose ZoomSearch, a training-free, plug-and-play pipeline that separates ‚Äòwhere to look‚Äô from ‚Äòhow to answer‚Äô. As illustrated in Fig.¬Ý1(i), ZoomSearch first performs a coarse-to-fine zoom-in search: starting from a fixed 3√ó33{\times}3 grid of the Ultra-HR image, it iteratively zooms into promising areas. At each level, an external scoring model¬Ý[visrag, remoteclip, GeoRSCLIP, lrsclip] estimates patch‚Äìtext relevance, while the foundation-model‚Äôs forward confidence provides a model-evidence signal. Candidates are then ranked by these two cues and expanded via an adaptive top-kk strategy to mitigate target dispersion. The selected regions are then reassembled into a compact, model-consumable canvas while preserving spatial cues at both local and global levels. The resulting canvas is finally submitted to a foundation model for answer prediction, without requiring fine-tuning or architectural changes.

Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

‚Ä¢

To mitigate the capacity‚Äìresolution mismatch in Ultra-HR RS-VQA, we formulate an explicit ZoomSearch paradigm that decouples ‚Äòwhere to look‚Äô from ‚Äòhow to answer‚Äô, enabling training-free adaptation of existing remote sensing foundation models to 2‚àº202{\sim}20K imagery.

‚Ä¢

To achieve this search paradigm in a plug-and-play manner, we design Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search and Layout-Aware Patch Reassembly. The former performs score-guided, coarse-to-fine zoom-in over image patches, while the latter reorganizes the selected regions into a compact canvas that preserves both local topology and global orientation for downstream RS-VQA.

‚Ä¢

We demonstrate the effectiveness of ZoomSearch on MME-RealWorld-RS and LRS-VQA benchmarks. When plugged into LLaVA-ov, it brings a substantial boost in average accuracy, with 26.3% improvement on LRS-VQA and an impressive 114.8% gain on MME-RealWorld-RS. At the same time, it reduces inference cost by 20‚àº\sim44% compared with existing search-based pipelines.

2 Related Work

2.1 Ultra-HR Remote Sensing VQA

Token Pruning-based Ultra-HR RS-VQA.
GeoLLaVA-8K¬Ý[geollava8k] proposes Background Token Pruning to merge redundant background areas and Anchored Token Selection to keep sparse informative objects. However, they both operate on pooled representations to control attention cost, inevitably losing fine details. Luo et al.¬Ý[lrsvqa] further introduce text-aware region localization with a coarse-to-fine patch selection scheme that avoids pooling. However, the coarse stage still operates on downsampled images, so pruning decisions are made at low resolution and may miss answer-critical structures, leading to cascading errors. Moreover, most pruning pipelines require additional trainable controllers or backbone modifications, which hinder plug-and-play use.

RAG-based Ultra-HR RS-VQA.
RAG methods¬Ý[rag] augment RS-VQA by retrieving semantically related image‚Äìtext pairs from external labeled Remote Sensing databases and injecting them as additional evidence. ImageRAG¬Ý[imagerag] uses RemoteCLIP¬Ý[remoteclip] variants to retrieve question-relevant patches and descriptions, which are fed to an RS-MLLM to better handle large, information-dense scenes. RS-RAG¬Ý[rsrag] constructs a Remote Sensing World Knowledge corpus and retrieves top-ranked image‚Äìtext knowledge into knowledge-augmented prompts. However, such approaches depend strongly on the coverage and quality of the external database, which limit the robustness and scalability in practice.

2.2 Search-Based VQA Methods

In natural-image VQA, several methods adopt a locate-then-answer paradigm¬Ý[visualcot, hrrl, dualfocus, DyFo, dc2, vstar, zoomeye]. SEAL¬Ý[vstar] performs an LLM-guided visual search that iteratively finds missing targets and stores cropped regions in a visual working memory for answer generation. ZoomEye¬Ý[zoomeye] represents an image as a hierarchical tree of zoomed patches and learns a policy to traverse this tree. Retrieval-Augmented Perception (RAP)¬Ý[rap] instead treats Ultra-HR VQA as a long-context problem and retrieves only query-relevant crops for answering, trading off context length against coverage.

These search-based approaches show that focusing computation on query-relevant regions is effective for high-resolution natural images. However, remote sensing imagery typically exhibits higher resolutions, cluttered backgrounds, and object-scale variation, calling for a more flexible search strategy. In this context, we propose ZoomSearch, a training-free, plug-and-play framework that operationalizes the principle of looking where it matters for Ultra-HR RS-VQA.

3 Pilot Study

In this section, we conduct a preliminary empirical investigation of the key challenges in designing a search-based framework for Ultra-HR RS-VQA. Specifically, we aim to address two questions: (i) what is an appropriate search unit for Ultra-HR imagery; and (ii) once relevant regions are identified, how should they be reassembled for answer prediction by a foundation model. The insights directly inform the final design of our ZoomSearch pipeline presented in the Sec.¬Ý4.

3.2 Exploring Proper Search Units

To study what constitutes an appropriate search unit for Ultra-HR remote sensing imagery, we compare three search policies: (i) a fine-tile policy, (ii) a coarse-patch policy and (iii) a hierarchical policy. In all cases, the search stage only selects the top-11 patch and feeds it to the foundation model for answer prediction.

We report per-category and average accuracy on the LRS-VQA subset in Fig.¬Ý2(i).
Overall, the hierarchical policy achieves the highest average performance, with particularly large gains on categories that depend on fine object geometry, such as shape and status. This indicates that progressively zooming from coarse to fine scales is better suited to capturing multi-scale targets in Ultra-HR remote sensing scenes.

3.3 Exploring Reassembly Strategies

We next investigate how the informative patches should be organized before being fed into the foundation model. In contrast to Sec.¬Ý3.2, all methods here use
the hierarchical search policy and select the top-kk patches for each question, which are
then reassembled by one of the following strategies:
(i) sequential concatenation,
(ii) relative-layout preserving and
(iii) relative & global-layout preserving.

Based on Fig.¬Ý2(ii), the relative & global-layout preserving strategy clearly performs best, achieving the highest accuracy on almost all categories. This suggests that, when multiple informative patches have been located, keeping both their mutual spatial relations and their coarse locations within the original image helps the foundation model better exploit spatial cues after reassembly.

4.1 Method Overview

Building on the insights from pilot studies in Sec.¬Ý3, we design
ZoomSearch to explicitly decouple where to look from how to answer
for Ultra-HR RS-VQA. Instead of feeding the entire Ultra-HR image directly
into a foundation model, ZoomSearch first progressively zooms into the scene to
localize question-relevant regions. It then replaces the full-resolution input with a
compact, layout-faithful representation of these regions, which serves as the visual
context for answer prediction. Concretely, we first employ Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search to perform a hierarchical exploration that identifies informative patches at appropriate scales. We then apply Layout-Aware Patch Reassembly to reorganize and compress the selected patches while preserving both local and global spatial relationships. The two components are detailed in the following subsections.

4.2 Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search

As indicated in Sec.¬Ý3.2, hierarchical exploration over image patches is more effective than single-scale search for Ultra-HR RS-VQA. Building on this observation, we design a training-free search procedure, termed Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search, which performs score-guided coarse-to-fine exploration to localize informative regions before answering. The complete algorithm is given in Sec.¬ÝS8.4, while representative search cases are illustrated in the top-left of Fig.¬Ý3 and in panel (i) of Fig.¬Ý1.

The root is the whole image; a node at depth dd is a patch that spawns nine children via a 3√ó33{\times}3 split. The goal is to output the selected-patch set ùí´sel\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{sel}} that covers the query-relevant regions.

4.4 Scoring Mechanism

For each candidate patch tt, ZoomSearch computes a scalar score by combining
(i) a patch‚Äìtext relevance term derived from an external scoring model and
(ii) a model-evidence signal obtained from the foundation model. We adopt VISRAG¬Ý[visrag] as the scoring model, and the overall
pipeline is illustrated in the bottom part of Fig.¬Ý3.

Patch‚Äìtext relevance.
Given a patch tt and question qq, we first decompose tt into a set of fixed-size tiles
{vi}i=1n\{v_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}, where each viv_{i} has spatial size umin√óuminu_{\min}\!\times u_{\min}.
The scoring model ‚Ñõ\mathcal{R} is a vision‚Äìlanguage model trained to estimate
the similarity between a text query and a image unit.
It independently encodes qq and each viv_{i} into embedding vectors

(6)

We then sort the tiles by s‚Äã(q,vi)s(q,v_{i}) and discard the lower 50% by replacing them with
gray padding, retaining the upper 50% as potentially relevant evidence.
Remote sensing imagery often contains large homogeneous backgrounds and other redundant
content; keeping only the more relevant half prevents such regions from dominating the

5 Experiments

5.1 Experimental Setting

We evaluate ZoomSearch on two Ultra-HR RS-VQA benchmarks: LRS-VQA¬Ý[lrsvqa] and MME-RealWorld-RS¬Ý[mmerealworld]. LRS-VQA contains satellite and aerial images whose longest side ranges from 10241024 to 2732827328 pixels, and includes eight categories of open-ended questions. MME-RealWorld-RS consists of real-world Ultra-HR remote sensing images with longest side between 689689 and 1150011500 pixels, paired with three types of multiple-choice
questions.

Our baselines cover four groups of models.
(i) Closed-source Foundation Models: Gemini-2.5-Flash¬Ý[gemini] and GPT-4o¬Ý[gpt4o].
(ii) Open-source general-purpose Foundation Models: LLaVA-1.5¬Ý[llava], LLaVA-1.6¬Ý[llavanext], LLaVA-ov¬Ý[llavaov], Qwen2.5-VL¬Ý[qwen2.5], and LLaVA-HR¬Ý[llamavid] (specifically trained for higher input resolutions).
(iii) Remote-sensing Foundation Models: GeoChat¬Ý[geochat] and VHM¬Ý[vhm].
(iv) Ultra-HR RS-VQA methods: GeoLLaVA-8K¬Ý[geollava8k] (token-pruning based) and ImageRAG¬Ý[imagerag] (RAG-based).
(v) Search-based Plug-and-play Methods: ZoomEye¬Ý[zoomeye] and RAP¬Ý[rap]. To enable a fair comparison, we build ZoomEye, RAP, and our ZoomSearch on the same base model‚ÄîLLaVA-onevision-qwen2-7b.

5.2 Qualitative Results

Figs.¬Ý4 and¬Ý5 compare ZoomSearch with state-of-the-art plug-and-play search-based methods on color recognition and counting tasks, respectively. In Fig.¬Ý4, our method localizes the bottom-most roundabout and the vehicle outside it, and produces the correct color answer, whereas both baselines focus on
irrelevant road segments and fail to answer.
In the counting example of Fig.¬Ý5, ZoomEye assigns the highest confidence to the full image itself, causing a failure search. RAP does hit the target region, but simultaneously retrieves many distractors, leading to the final wrong count. In contrast, ZoomSearch precisely zooms into the correct target area, yielding the correct answer. More qualitative examples are provided in panel (i) of Fig.¬Ý1, in Fig.¬Ý3, and in Sec.¬ÝS10.1. Overall, these cases highlight two key advantages of our design. First, the hierarchical, adaptive zoom search effectively handles the strong multi-scale nature of remote sensing targets, enabling robust localization of tiny objects in Ultra-HR scenes. Second, the layout-aware reassembly preserves critical spatial cues, which is crucial for queries that depend on fine-grained positional relations.

6 Conclusion

We introduced ZoomSearch, a training-free, plug-and-play framework for Ultra-HR RS-VQA that (i) conducts Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search to localize query-relevant regions via coarse-to-fine exploration, and (ii) performs Layout-Aware Patch Reassembly to build a compact canvas that preserves both inter-patch relations and global orientation. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial accuracy gains with faster inference speed than existing methods. These benefits arise from focusing computation where it matters through hierarchical zooming, while retaining critical spatial cues under a low visual-token budget. Looking ahead, we plan to further improve both the intelligence and efficiency of the search process for Ultra-HR RS-VQA.

S7 Pilot Study: Extended Setup and Analyses

Setup.

We construct a 2,000-sample subset from LRS-VQA by first filtering images whose longest side exceeds 2K pixels and then sampling according to the original task distribution. The base model is llava-onevision-qwen2-0.5b. Unless noted, the scoring and search hyper-parameters follow the main paper.

Exploring proper search units.

Table¬Ý8 contrasts three policies.
The fine-tile policy fixes the tile size at 224√ó224224{\times}224 and selects the single best tile according to our scoring mechanism.
The coarse-patch policy partitions the image once into a 3√ó33{\times}3 grid and selects the best cell.
The hierarchical policy repeatedly splits the current patch into a 3√ó33{\times}3 grid and zooms in guided by the score, stopping when the evidence threshold is met or the unit size falls to uminu_{\min}.
All three policies outperform the backbone (Avg. 24.59%), confirming that explicitly looking where it matters is crucial for Ultra-HR inputs.
Among them, the hierarchical policy provides the most favorable overall balance: relative to fine-tile, it increases average accuracy by 4.0% while reducing latency by 62.4%; relative to coarse-patch, it yields a further 7.6% accuracy improvement at a moderate time cost, reflecting a controlled accuracy‚Äìefficiency trade-off.

At the category level, the hierarchical strategy delivers clear gains on Status, and Shape, consistent with its ability to resolve tiny but answer-critical structures.
An exception is Color, for which coarse-patch attains the highest score (49.52%); this suggests that chromatic cues are often better captured at coarser spatial scales, whereas excessive zooming may over-emphasize local textures and introduce noise.

Exploring reassembly strategies.

We first establish the search protocol: hierarchical exploration with an adaptive Top-kk branching strategy using a fixed post-sigmoid threshold of 0.60.6. We subsequently compare three strategies for composing the selected patches.
First, sequential concatenation encodes patches in raster order and concatenates their tokens.
Second, relative-layout preserving follows RAP¬Ý[rap]: it removes only all-zero rows and columns across patches so that pairwise spatial relations are maintained.
Third, relative & global-layout preserving anchors each coarse region to a fixed quadrant to preserve global orientation, and then applies intra-region compression to preserve the relative layout.

Across task categories, the relative & global strategy attains the highest average accuracy (28.38%) and yields consistent gains, including Reasoning 26.01%, Status 13.48%, Shape 37.05%, Color 44.59%, and Background 20.32%.
These findings suggest that combining quadrant-level anchoring with preservation of intra-region order provides complementary cues, thereby retaining task-relevant information more effectively than alternative layouts.

S8.4 Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search

Overview and link to tree search.
Our procedure follows the spirit of A*: we keep an open list (priority queue) ùí™\mathcal{O}, rank candidates by a fused score s^\hat{s} (analogous to an ff-score), and iteratively expand the best frontier while committing finished nodes to ùí´sel\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{sel}}. The score combines two cues, gg and hh (Sec.¬ÝS8.3), and a sigmoid normalization makes a fixed threshold behave adaptively within each set of sibling patches.

S9 Extended Experiments

We provide additional experiments to further validate the design and generality of ZoomSearch.
Specifically, we (i) ablate the two core components‚ÄîAdaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search and Layout-Aware Patch Reassembly;
(ii) assess plug-and-play compatibility with a variety of base models; and
(iii) examine robustness to different scoring models.
Unless otherwise noted, all experiments are conducted on the same 2,000-sample subset of LRS-VQA used in the pilot study, with llava-onevision-qwen2-7B as the default backbone.

S9.1 Ablations on the Two Core Components

To evaluate the roles of our two principal design components, we examine each in isolation before assessing their combined effect.
As reported in Table¬Ý10, using Adaptive Multi-Branch Zoom Search on its own leads to an average improvement of 12.52%. In this setting, the search mechanism is preserved but the selected crops are simply arranged sequentially without preserving the spatial layout.
We then isolate Layout-Aware Patch Reassembly by selecting patches solely according to patch‚Äìtext relevance and subsequently reconstructing them with the layout-aware strategy. This configuration yields a slightly larger gain of 13.74%.
When both components are activated together, the average accuracy rises to 24.22%. This joint improvement demonstrates that the hierarchical search procedure and the spatially faithful reassembly mechanism contribute complementary strengths, and that their integration is essential to realizing the full capability of ZoomSearch.

S9.2 Plug-and-Play Across Different Base Models

We evaluate ZoomSearch as a plug-and-play front end across diverse backbones‚Äîtwo remote sensing foundation models (GeoChat, VHM) and several general-purpose MLLMs from the LLaVA and Qwen-VL families. We report the results in Tab.¬Ý11. Across all backbones, ZoomSearch improves the average accuracy: for example, gains of +6.51% for GeoChat, +7.15% for VHM, +7.14% for Qwen2.5-VL-7b, +12.12% for LLaVA-v1.6-7b, and +24.22% for LLaVA-ov-7b are observed. The improvements are particularly pronounced on fine-grained categories (Category, Shape, Color), suggesting that concentrating tokens on query-relevant regions is especially beneficial for detail-oriented reasoning.

The magnitude of improvement correlates with backbone quality. Stronger models (e.g., LLaVA-ov-7b, LLaVA-v1.6-7b) provide more reliable model-evidence signals for early stopping and branching, and thus realize larger overall gains. In contrast, LLaVA-v1.5-7b exhibits higher variance in the evidence signal and struggles to answer correctly even when the search localizes the right area, leading to a modest net gain. For remote sensing backbones, we observe solid average improvements despite a mild decline on Rural/urban; this likely reflects a distribution shift introduced by our compact canvas relative to the models‚Äô pretraining imagery for scene-level classification. Excluding this scene-type artifact, ZoomSearch consistently enhances object- and attribute-centric tasks, underscoring the method‚Äôs effectiveness and robustness across architectures and domains.

S9.3 Ablation on Scoring Models

To compute the patch-text relevance term g‚Äã(t)g(t), we replace the scoring model ‚Ñõ\mathcal{R} with four alternatives‚ÄîRemoteCLIP¬Ý[remoteclip], GeoRSCLIP¬Ý[GeoRSCLIP], LRSCLIP¬Ý[lrsclip], and VisRAG¬Ý[visrag]‚Äîwhile keeping the search/reassembly pipeline fixed. As shown in Table¬Ý12, all variants yield consistent improvements over the backbone (‚âà22%\approx 22\%). Overall, VisRAG attains the highest average accuracy, but the margins are modest, suggesting that ZoomSearch is robust to the specific choice of ‚Ñõ\mathcal{R}. Unless otherwise noted, we therefore adopt VisRAG for main results.

S10 Additional Results

S10.1 Additional Qualitative Examples

Figure¬Ý6 presents four representative Ultra-HR RS-VQA cases.
(I) and (III) depict dense urban scenes with minute targets. ZoomSearch first localizes the yellow crane and the tennis court, respectively; layout-aware reassembly then anchors each region to its original quadrant, preserving global placement. This preservation of absolute orientation is crucial for answering directional queries (e.g., ‚Äúupper right area,‚Äù ‚Äúto the right above the picture‚Äù), which are prevalent in remote sensing tasks that require image-referenced positioning, including incident reporting, infrastructure inspection, and asset monitoring.
(II) considers a counting scenario, in which ZoomSearch successfully localizes all tanks. By preserving each patch‚Äôs absolute position in the original frame, the reassembled canvas renders spatial descriptors such as ‚Äúbottom part of the image‚Äù unambiguous during answering.
(IV) involves a low-contrast lattice tower amid clutter. Hierarchical zooming concentrates evidence on the subtle structure, and the final layout-faithful canvas supports a precise prediction.

Figure¬Ý7 illustrates the ZoomSearch pipeline for a query about the white tower‚Äôs location. Depth-1 heatmaps already highlight the correct neighborhood, though some tiles receive spurious high scores. As the search continues, hierarchical zooming and progressive pruning suppress these false positives and strengthen evidence around the true region. Although the object spans two tiles at Depth 2, the adaptive Top-kk policy keeps both branches, ensuring full coverage. The final layout-aware canvas preserves global orientation and local spatial relations, enabling an accurate answer.

Figure 6: Qualitative results of ZoomSearch. In both examples, the method successfully zooms into extremely fine-grained target objects in Ultra-HR remote sensing images and produces correct answers.

Figure 7: Overview of the ZoomSearch pipeline. Given an Ultra-HR remote sensing image and a query, the procedure (i) scores a 3√ó33{\times}3 grid and prunes 50% low-confidence tiles, (ii) performs hierarchical expansion with adaptive Top-kk retention, and (iii) reassembles the selected patches into a compact, layout-faithful canvas that preserves both global orientation and intra-region spatial relations. The example illustrates localization of the white tower and construction of the final canvas.

S10.2 Failure Cases and Analysis

Figure¬Ý8 highlights two recurrent failure modes observed in our evaluation.
(I) Backbone‚Äìlimited answering. The search correctly isolates the target region, and the reassembly preserves both its global orientation and intra-region spatial relations, yet the base VLM still produces an incorrect answer. In such cases the visual evidence is present and well framed, but the foundation model‚Äôs recognition or reasoning is insufficient. This suggests an upper bound imposed by the backbone rather than the search policy. (II) Incomplete or imperfect search. The procedure occasionally misses a small, relevant instance (under-coverage) or retains a distractor (over-coverage). The example shows one red roof on the far left not selected at Depth 3, leading to an undercount. Notably, the layout anchors patches to their original quadrants. Consequently, the instruction ‚Äòbottom right corner‚Äô constrains the backbone to count only within the bottom-right canvas block. This constraint mitigates the error, although it does not eliminate it entirely.

Future work may explore (i) more robust search policies that better balance coverage and precision under Ultra-HR remote sensing complexity, (ii) lightweight self-verification modules that assess whether the retrieved patches adequately cover the query-relevant evidence‚Äîthereby reducing error accumulation from imperfect search, and (iii) learned scoring mechanisms that couple retrieval signals more tightly with the backbone‚Äôs internal representations.
